title: Lace-effect negative space cutting
subtitle: Cutting intricate linked patterns in paper using a scalpel and light box
excerpt: Negative-space papercutting removes material from a single flat sheet to leave a connected web of paper that resembles lace when held to light. Unlike folded cutting, each cut is made individually to a planned design. The scalpel and light box allow precise work on thin paper; the design must be engineered so that every element remains connected to at least two others.
sourceNotes: Negative-space papercutting technique synthesised from scherenschnitte, jianzhi, and general papercutting practice.

glossaryTerms.definition: The German and Swiss tradition of decorative paper cutting, literally 'scissors cutting'. Both folded and flat forms; often silhouette figures, birds, and botanical forms. The flat lace-cutting tradition is closely related.
In lace papercutting design, the requirement that every element of the design must connect to at least two other elements, so the sheet holds together as a single piece when all the cuts are made.

content.text: in the German and Swiss tradition, is the most demanding form of flat papercutting. Every design decision has structural effects: an element too thin will tear, and an element left uncut reads as a solid area that breaks the lace effect. Design and cutting cannot be separated. You must think about structure before you cut.
Draw the design on tracing paper first. Mark not only the positive shapes (lines and forms) but also the negative spaces (areas to be cut away). Check the

: every positive element must touch at least two other positive elements. If a shape touches only one, it will come loose when the cut is complete. If in doubt, add a thin bridge (a 1 to 2 mm strip of paper) connecting the isolated element to a neighbour.

Tape the tracing-paper design to the light box. Lay the cutting paper over it. The design should be clearly visible through the top sheet. Secure both layers with small pieces of masking tape at the corners. Do not tape across the design area: you will need to cut through the tape, which dulls the blade.

Cut the smallest, most detailed elements first, working from the centre of the design outward. The paper is most rigid and least likely to tear before the large surrounding areas are removed. Cut each closed shape with a single continuous movement where possible: lifting and re-entering a curve creates a visible join. For straight lines, use a metal ruler and a single-pass cut, the ruler is a guide and a safety edge.

A scalpel blade cuts cleanly for about 15, 20 minutes of intensive papercutting. A dull blade drags and tears the paper. Change the blade as soon as you feel it catching rather than cutting. For a full A4 lace-cutting session, expect to use two or three blades. Dispose of used blades in a sharps container, not loose in the bin.
